9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A few months ago, I was trying to answer what should have been a simple question:
How do you actually build a career in AI security?
Not AI security in the abstract. I mean the real, emerging discipline of securing AI systems themselves: attacking LLMs, hunting vulnerabilities in agentic pipelines, red teaming model infrastructure, and auditing RAG implementations for data exfiltration risks.
This is the field that barely had a defined career track two years ago. Now it has job postings that almost nobody is fully qualified for.
The honest answer I kept running into was this: nobody has completely figured it out yet.
The field is moving faster than the certification bodies, faster than the job descriptions, and faster than the curricula. But that does not mean there is no path. It means the path has to be constructed more deliberately than most security career tracks.
Here is what I worked out, and why I think most people are approaching this the wrong way.
As always, if you want a visual breakdown; check out my video below:
The First Mistake: Treating AI Security as One Thing
When I enter a new field, the instinct is always to look for the canonical certification. The OSCP equivalent. The credential that tells the market, “this person knows what they are doing.”
That instinct works in established areas of cybersecurity.
In AI security, I think it leads people in the wrong direction because AI security is not one thing.
Look at the difference between these two roles.
First, there is the application security engineer securing AI-powered products. This person is reviewing LLM integrations for prompt injection risks, auditing how retrieval-augmented generation pipelines handle sensitive data, and assessing the attack surface of AI agents with tool-calling capabilities.
That person needs to understand AI systems as an application security target. Their mental model is defensive-first, with enough offensive knowledge to know how these systems break.
Then there is the AI red teamer specifically hired to attack AI infrastructure. This person is fingerprinting models, chaining indirect prompt injections across multi-agent systems, probing MCP server security, and attempting to extract sensitive data through carefully constructed inference attacks.
That person needs OSCP-level offensive methodology applied to a new attack surface. Their mental model is offensive-first. No ambiguity.
Same field. Completely different preparation requirements.
That is why I think trying to find one certification that serves both paths is the wrong frame.
The right question is not:
What is the best AI security certification?
The right question is:
What role am I actually trying to grow into, and what does that role require?
Once I reframed the problem that way, the roadmap became much clearer.
Starting From Zero
If I were entering AI security without a background in either offensive security or machine learning, I would treat that as a prerequisite problem first. No certification can magically erase that gap.
The advanced AI security programs, especially the ones with practical exams and real employer recognition, usually assume that you already have one of two things: penetration testing experience, AI and machine learning fundamentals, or ideally both.
Walking into a 24-hour proctored AI red-team exam without that foundation is not ambition. It is expensive frustration.
For someone starting from this position, the first credential I would look at is CompTIA SecAI+.
But I want to be clear about what it is and what it is not.
SecAI+ is a recognizable, brand-name credential that helps establish baseline vocabulary around AI security concepts, AI risk, and governance. The exam is multiple choice and performance-based, with a maximum of 60 questions. It gives you a structured introduction to the conceptual landscape of AI security, and that matters because the more advanced material becomes much easier to absorb once the terminology is no longer foreign.
But SecAI+ will not teach you to attack anything. It is not enough by itself.
It is theoretical enough that the practical skills it develops are limited. You will not get hired as an AI red teamer simply because you hold SecAI+. I would think of it as the Security+ of the AI security world: useful, recognizable, and a good signal that you have started taking the field seriously, but not a destination.
If you already have security fundamentals and you mainly need to bridge into AI-specific knowledge before attempting the more advanced exams, there is another course I would seriously consider: the Certified AI Security Expert program from Modern Security.
What caught my attention about this program is the learning arc. You first build AI systems. Then you attack them. Then you defend them.
If you understand how RAG pipelines are constructed before trying to exploit them, the attack logic becomes intuitive instead of memorized. If you understand embeddings, vector databases, agent design, tool calling, and MCP server architecture from the builder’s side, then prompt injection, data exposure, insecure integrations, and agent abuse stop feeling like disconnected tricks.
They start looking like natural consequences of how the system is designed.
The program covers AI and LLM fundamentals, embeddings, vector databases, LangChain, MCP server architecture, AI agent design, and hands-on labs for attacks such as prompt injection, data exposure, and insecure integrations. It does not require prior AI experience, although Python basics will make the process easier.
For an application security engineer, developer, or security professional trying to understand the full AI security lifecycle before committing to a 24-hour practical exam, I think this is the right preparation layer.
I would place it clearly in the roadmap hierarchy.
This is a practical bridge course that teaches AI security from both perspectives: offensive and defensive. It includes hands-on labs and coding, and I would absolutely add it to LinkedIn. More importantly, I would use it as a structured way to build portfolio projects.
It is also worth mentioning that the course author has presented at Black Hat and DEF CON, and runs the course in conference settings such as NorthSec. That does not automatically make the course the right fit for everyone, but it does add credibility to the material and the way it is positioned.
So my sequence for someone starting from zero would be simple:
Build the security foundation first. Use SecAI+ to establish the vocabulary. Use a practical AI security course to understand how these systems are built, broken, and defended. Only then start thinking seriously about advanced AI red-team certifications.
The OSCP Parallel: Why Offensive Security AI 300 Is the Benchmark
When offensive security launched OSCP, they did something that changed how the industry thinks about penetration testing certifications. They made the exam a 24-hour practical exercise where you either compromise machines or you don’t.You demonstrate the skill or you fail.
Offensive Security AI 300 , OSAI , applies that same philosophy to AI systems. The 24-hour proctored exam simulates a realistic AI-enabled enterprise environment. The curriculum treats LLMs, agent frameworks, and model infrastructure the same way an OSCP-level tester treats a corporate network: as a target surface to be systematically enumerated, exploited, and documented.
The syllabus covers reconnaissance against AI targets, model fingerprinting, evasion techniques, prompt injection and jailbreaking, indirect injection chains, multi-agent system exploitation, RAG pipeline attacks, data exfiltration through embeddings, MCP protocol attacks, cloud API and model hosting vulnerabilities, and supply chain attacks against AI components. Reporting and documentation for AI red team engagements is included because the deliverable in a real engagement isn’t just access, it’s a report that explains what you found and how.
Here’s what I found compelling about this and why I think it’s the benchmark credential for the offensive path: the 24-hour practical exam is a trust signal that other certifications don’t produce. Employers who understand offensive security know that the format eliminates the possibility of passing without actually being able to do the work. That’s why OSCP holders get hired on brand reputation alone in some markets. OSAI inherits that trust from the same exam philosophy.
The honest requirement: this certification assumes you’ve already mastered conventional penetration testing methodology. OSCP or equivalent hands-on experience is explicitly recommended, not optional lip service. If you don’t have that foundation, the 24-hour exam will expose the gap in the most expensive way possible. This is not the credential to attempt if you’re coming in from a purely defensive background without red team experience.
And a clarification that matters for career planning: OSAI is purely offensive. There is no defensive AI security component in this certification. You will not use it to build secure AI systems or establish governance frameworks. This is the right credential if your target role is AI penetration testing, AI red teaming operations, or offensive security consulting where client trust requires the OffSec brand behind your findings.
HTB Certified Offensive AI Expert
HackTheBox launched their Certified Offensive AI Expert in April 2026, developed in collaboration with Google and explicitly aligned with Google’s Secure AI Framework. This was notable to me for a specific reason: institutional collaboration adds credibility that brand recognition alone can’t fully provide, especially for a certificate that’s new enough that not every hiring manager has encountered it yet.
The technical depth here is meaningfully different from OSAI. Where Offensive Security teaches you to attack AI-powered applications and infrastructure, HTB AI Expert goes considerably deeper into the model layer itself ; adversarial machine learning theory, neural network architecture, model evasion mathematics. The difference in focus: OSAI teaches you to attack systems that use AI. HTB AI Expert teaches you to attack the AI models themselves.
This distinction matters more than it might initially appear. Attacking a RAG pipeline for data exfiltration is an application security problem that requires understanding how RAG works. Attacking a model through adversarial inputs, membership inference attacks, or model inversion requires understanding how the model learns. These are different skill sets that call for different preparation and serve different roles.
The exam format is seven days with a professional technical report requirement. The ML/AI foundations requirement is more explicit here than in OSAI ; data scientists transitioning into security, or security engineers with meaningful ML background, will absorb this material more naturally. The seven-day practical format produces something concrete that employers can point to, even if brand recognition continues to build as the certificate population grows.
How to Actually Choose
After spending weeks thinking through this, here’s the framework I landed on:
If you’re starting from zero with no offensive security or AI background: Security AI Plus for vocabulary, then the Modern Security CASE program for foundational AI security knowledge with hands-on labs, then reassess which advanced path matches where you want to go. Don’t skip the foundational layer to jump straight to a 24-hour exam.
If you’re a penetration tester or red teamer looking to extend into AI: Skip the foundational course and go directly to OSAI. Your offensive methodology already transfers ; what you need is to apply it to a new attack surface, which is exactly what the AI 300 curriculum delivers. OSAI will also carry the brand trust you need when clients are evaluating enga
